elements.sent-2411-001: For every integer $k\geq 2$ and every $R>1$ one can find a dimension $n$ and construct a symmetric convex body $K\subset\R^n$ with $\diam Q_{k-1 (K)\geq R\cdot\diam Q_k(K)$, where $Q_k(K)$ denotes the $k$-convex hull of $K$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: The purpose of this short note is to show that this result due to E.\ Kopeck\'{a is impossible to obtain if one additionally requires that all isometric images of $K$ satisfy the same inequality.}
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we introduce the dual construction to the $k$-convex hull of $K$, which we call $k$-cross approximation.
elements.sent-2411-004: Let $\{e_1,\dots,e_n\}$ be the standard basis of $\R^n$ and, for every $S\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}$, let us denote by $\func{P_S {\R^n {\R^n}$ the orthogonal projection onto the subspace spanned by $\{e_j\,:\,j\in S\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: For every $k\in\{1,\dots,n\}$, let us call ${[n] _k$ the family of all subsets $S\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}$ with cardinality $\# S=k$.}
elements.sent-2411-006: For every convex body $K\subset\R^n$, the $k$-convex hull of $K$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:kconvex} Q_k(K)=\bigcap_{S\in{[n]}_k}P_S^{-1}\bigl(P_S(K)\bigr).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-007: The reason behind this terminology is easily explained.
elements.sent-2411-008: Given a positive integer $n$ and $k\in\{1,\dots,n\}$, we say that a convex body $K\subset\R^n$ is $k$-convex if every $x\in\partial K$ has an outward normal vector with at most $k$ nonzero coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-009: Alternatively, $K$ is $k$-convex if it is the intersection of a family $\mathcal{F}$ of half spaces such that the boundary of every $H\in\mathcal{F}$ is a hyperplane defined by at most $k$ coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is clear that the intersection of an arbitrary family of $k$-convex bodies is again $k$-convex.
elements.sent-2411-011: This enables us to consider the $k$-convex hull of a convex body $K\subset\R^n$, i.e., the intersection of all $k$-convex bodies containing $K$.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is not difficult to check that the $k$-convex hull of $K$ is given precisely by (\ref{eqn:kconvex ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: The $1$-convex hull $Q_1(K)$ is the smallest box aligned with the standard orthonormal system that contains $K$.
elements.sent-2411-014: The family ${\{Q_k(K)\} _{k=1 ^n$ forms a nested sequence of sets which approximates $K$ from above.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: At the final degree of approximation we have $Q_n(K)=K$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, if $K$ is symmetric so are all its $k$-convex hulls.
elements.sent-2411-017: The original interest in $k$-convex sets lies in the study of contractive sets, that is, the ranges of $1$-Lipschitz retractions.
elements.sent-2411-018: In $\ell_2$ spaces, all closed convex sets are contractive.
elements.sent-2411-019: In $\ell_p$ spaces with $p\in(1,2)\cup(2,\infty)$, contractive sets are precisely the $2$-convex sets (see \cite{LH, DE, E ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: In $\ell_1$, the family of contractive sets contains all $2$-convex sets, but it is larger \cite{W .}
elements.sent-2411-021: If the dimension of the space is allowed to be arbitrarily large, then the diameter of $Q_1(K)$ can be arbitrarily larger than the one of $K=Q_n(K)$, as the example of a Euclidean unit ball shows.
elements.sent-2411-022: Surprisingly, not only is this true when one compares $Q_1(K)$ and $K$.

elements.sent-2411-024: We may also measure the involved diameters with respect to any $p$-norm with $p\in[1,\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-025: If $K\subset\R^n$ is one of the convex bodies whose existence is predicted by Theorem \ref{thm:kopecka , it might well be that, for some $U\in\SO(n)$, the body $UK$ fails to satisfy the inequality of Theorem \ref{thm:kopecka , even though, at least in the Euclidean norm, $K$ and $UK$ are isometric.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In a private exchange with the author, E.\ Kopeck\'{a brought up the following question.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Does there exist an absolute constant $C>0$ such that, for every integer $n\geq 2$ and every symmetric convex body $K\subset\R^n$, there is $U\in\SO(n)$ with \begin{equation}\frac{\diam{Q_1(UK)}}{\diam{Q_2(UK)}}\leq C\,?\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this note, we provide an affirmative answer by proving something stronger.
elements.sent-2411-029: In the second step of the proof, we describe a simple greedy algorithm to find a positively oriented orthonormal basis $\{u_1,\dots,u_n\}$ such that $R_k(UK)\subseteq 3R_{k-1 (UK)$ for every $k\in\{2,\dots,n\}$, where $U\in\SO(n)$ is the isometry of $\R^n$ which maps $u_i$ to $e_i$ for every $i\in\{1,\dots,n\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: By applying this procedure to the body $K^\circ$, the polar body of $K$, we are then able to obtain Theorem \ref{thm:main .}

elements.section-2411-002: Cross approximations of a convex body
elements.sent-2411-042: Given an integer $n\geq 2$ and $S\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}$, let us denote by $F(S)$ the subspace of $\R^n$ spanned by $\{e_j\,:\,j\in S\}$.
elements.sent-2411-043: For every $k\in\{1,\dots,n\}$ and every symmetric convex body $K\subset\R^n$ we set \begin{equation}\label{eqn:cross} R_k(K)=\conv\biggl(K\cap\bigcup_{S\in{[n]}_k}F(S)\biggr)\end{equation} and call $R_k(K)$ the $k$-cross approximation of $K$.
elements.sent-2411-044: As in the case of $k$-convex hulls, the family ${\{R_k(K)\} _{k=1 ^n$ forms a nested sequence of convex bodies which approximate $K$ more and more precisely, this time from below, until we get $R_n(K)=K$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The duality relation between $k$-convex hulls and $k$-cross approximations is given by the following lemma.

elements.sent-2411-053: The statement is now the result of the following simple computations involving polarity. \begin{equation}{Q_k(K)}^\circ ={\biggl(\,\bigcap_{S\in{[n]}_k}P_S^{-1}\bigl(P_S(K)\bigr)\biggr)}^\circ = \conv\biggl(\,\bigcup_{S\in{[n]}_k}{P_S^{-1}\bigl(P_S(K)\bigr)}^\circ\biggr) = \conv\biggl(\,\bigcup_{S\in{[n]}_k}K^\circ\cap F(S)\biggr)=R_k(K^\circ). \qedhere\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-136: We briefly recall some notation and terminology that we will use throughout the paper.

elements.sent-2411-147: Let us start with two technical lemmas, which we prove directly in a general Hilbert space.

elements.sent-2411-198: We conclude with a remark regarding an alternative construction of the $k$-convex hull in infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces.
